title: practical english usage - Part 263
content:
the third day of the course - Timetable for Day Three Mozart's thirty-ninth symphony - Symphony No. 39, by Mozart the fourth book - Book Four the third act - Act Three ordinal number (first, second, etc). This structure is common in tides. Compare: After a noun we  usually use a cardinal number (one, two, etc) instead of an kings    and   queens 6 cardinal      and   ordinal      numbers:      books,    chapters,      etc; 9  IX ix        30   XXXxxx      1995 MCMXCV R   VIII viii      21   XXIxxi       1000  M XX 7  VII vii       20  XX         500  D 6  VI vi        19   XIXxix      100    C c XC 5 V  iv          14   XIV xiv       90     XC 1   IV iv        13   XIII xiii      60      LX lx 3  III iii        12  XII xii       50     L I < 2  II ii         11   XI xi        45    XLV xlv 1   I i          10  X  A                                          40      XL xl The  Roman numerals normally used are as follows: a fine XVIII Century English walnut chest of drawers Do question (vi) or question (vii), but not both.

For details, see Introduction page ix.

It was built in the time of Henry V.

on some old clock faces, and occasionally the names of centuries.

some documents, the numbers of questions in some examinations, the figures numbers in the introductions to some books, the numbers of paragraphs in are still used in a few cases - for example the names of kings and queens, page Roman numerals (I, II, III, IV, etc) are not common in modern English, but they 5 Roman     numerals 307 4922  three oh/zero seven, four nine two two 0 is often called oh (like the letter O).

We   say each figure separately, pausing after groups of three or four (not two).

4 telephone        numbers One in three new cars break/breaks down in the first year.

and plural verbs are possible.

After expressions like one in three, one out of five + plural noun, both singular Vocabulary Areas  Section 29 vocabulary •  322   numbers not One of my friends gave me this. (     One my friend. ..) You're my one hope.

is necessary.

Numbers can be used after determiners. Before determiners, a structure with of 11   numbers       with    determiners (BUT a/one pound  one pound twenty-five) but an/one hour and seventeen minutes (     one hour, seventeen minutes) (BUT a/one kilometre one kilometre, six hundred metres) We   can use a or one with measurement words. The  rules are similar.

one thousand, six hundred and two (More natural than a thousand, six a/one thousand a/one thousand and forty-nine of hundreds. Compare: A thousand can be used alone, and before and, but not usually before a number not three thousand one hundred (     three thousand a hundred) a/one hundred A can only be used at the beginning of a number. Compare: He was fined one thousand pounds for dangerous driving.

I want to live for a hundred years. (     ... -for hundred years) one thousand, a million or one million, etc. One is more formal.

We   can say an eighth or one eighth, a hundred or one hundred, a thousand or 10 a  and   one thirty-six, forty-nine, etc.

Note the hyphen between the tens and units in twenty-one, twenty-two, There are 1 000 millimetres in a metre.

Spaces are also possible.

4,126    4126  the year 1648 dates.

We   do not always use commas in four-figure numbers, and they are not used in not 3,127  (     3.127)  5,466,243 not used in this way.

three figures, by separating off the thousands and the millions. Full stops (.) are In writing we  generally use commas (,) to divide large numbers into groups of two hours (and) ten minutes two metres (and) thirty centimetres smaller, but is usually left out.

In measurements containing two different units, and is possible before the 2,025  two thousand and twenty-five 5,642  five thousand, six hundred and forty-two 310    three hundred and ten (AmE  also three hundred ten) numbers below a hundred. In American English, and can be dropped.

In British English we  always put and between hundred/thousand/million and 9 and:    punctuation the British first floor is the American second floor, etc.
